food_subgroup: Soup noodle dishes with protein and vegetables

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_00002473 (noodle soup) is the closest parent class. No exact FoodOn term exists for this specific Singaporean deluxe soup noodle dish with chicken, seafood beancurd roll, fish tofu, and vegetables.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (noodle soup dishes) is the closest available parent category. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for this specific multi-ingredient Singaporean soup noodle dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for this specific composite Singaporean soup noodle dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of deluxe soup noodles (approximately 500 g) as typically served at a Singaporean noodle restaurant, comprising noodles, broth, chicken breast, seafood beancurd roll, fish tofu, shredded carrots, shredded radish, and mixed vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: Noodle soup with meat and vegetables — composite estimate based on HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB Foodo) and USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single HPB Foodo entry exactly matches this multi-ingredient deluxe soup noodle dish. Nutrient values are estimated by component-weighted composition: wheat noodles (~40% by weight), chicken breast (~15%), seafood beancurd roll and fish tofu (~15%), vegetables including carrots and radish (~20%), and soup broth (~10%). HPB Foodo entries used: 'Noodles in soup' (plain), 'Chicken breast, steamed/boiled', 'Fish tofu'. USDA SR Legacy used for carrot, radish, and broth components. Values represent a plausible per-100g composite for the assembled dish as served.

gi_source.title: Noodle soup (wheat noodles in broth) — analogous GI estimate based on 'Instant noodles, boiled' and 'Wheat noodles, boiled' entries from the University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific multi-ingredient Singaporean deluxe soup noodle dish. GI of 52 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entries for boiled wheat noodles (GI approximately 47–58, mean ~52) and noodle soup dishes. The dish is dominated by wheat noodles (~40% by weight), which are the primary glycemic driver. Protein from chicken and fish tofu, and dietary fibre from vegetables, are expected to moderately attenuate the overall glycemic response. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 52.5) / 100 = 27.3, based on 500 g serving with 10.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soup broth and processed seafood ingredients
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its estimated sodium content of approximately 420 mg per 100 g (approximately 2,100 mg per 500 g serving), which approaches or exceeds the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg; requesting reduced-sodium broth or consuming only half the soup is strongly advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from wheat noodles, partially offset by protein and fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that this dish has a moderate glycemic load (GL approximately 27 per serving) driven primarily by wheat noodles; portion control is recommended, and choosing a smaller noodle portion or substituting with lower-GI noodles such as glass noodles or konjac noodles may help manage postprandial blood glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from processed seafood ingredients and broth
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should note the high sodium content of this dish and the presence of processed seafood products (beancurd roll, fish tofu) which may contain added sodium and saturated fat; consuming this dish occasionally and requesting less broth or a lower-sodium preparation is advisable to support cardiovascular health.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Deluxe Soup Noodles with Chicken, Seafood Beancurd Roll, Fish Tofu, and Vegetables is a hearty Singaporean Chinese noodle soup dish served at casual dining noodle restaurants in Singapore. The dish features wheat noodles served in a savoury clear or light broth, topped with house-marinated chicken breast meat, seafood beancurd roll (a processed seafood product made from fish paste and beancurd skin), fish tofu (a soft, lightly seasoned fish-based tofu product), shredded carrots, shredded radish, and mixed vegetables. The noodles are typically boiled until tender and the chicken breast is marinated and either poached or steamed, resulting in a lean, moist protein component. The overall flavour profile is mild, savoury, and umami-rich, with the broth providing depth and the vegetables adding freshness and texture contrast. Nutritionally, the dish provides a moderate energy density of approximately 92 kcal per 100 g, with a meaningful protein contribution of approximately 7.2 g per 100 g from the chicken and seafood components. The carbohydrate content of approximately 10.5 g per 100 g is primarily from the wheat noodles, giving the dish a moderate glycemic index of approximately 52 (low GI category) and a moderate glycemic load per serving. The sodium content is elevated at approximately 420 mg per 100 g due to the soup broth and processed seafood ingredients, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is dairy-free and provides useful micronutrients including vitamin A from the carrots, vitamin B12 from the seafood components, and iron and zinc from the chicken and fish products. It is suitable as a main meal and is commonly consumed at lunch or dinner in Singapore.
common_user_queries: What is in deluxe soup noodles?
Is noodle soup good for diabetics?
Can I eat soup noodles if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No single approved source entry exactly matches this multi-ingredient deluxe soup noodle dish as served by The Noodle Place. Nutrient values are estimated by component-weighted composition using HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for the individual components. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using analogous boiled wheat noodle entries. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are approved primary sources and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy component sources used for this composite estimate. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed as negligible/trace in the component sources (chicken breast, fish tofu, vegetables) — no industrially produced trans fats expected in this dish. The dish is served by The Noodle Place at Circular Road, Singapore, at SGD 16.90. Halal status is not confirmed as the restaurant's halal certification status is not verified from the input data. The serving size of 500 g is an estimate for a standard restaurant bowl; actual weight may vary. Nutrient values should be verified against direct laboratory analysis or an exact HPB Foodo entry if one becomes available. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate; direct GI measurement for this specific dish is not available.
